system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 13: Symetric Harmony

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 13 — Symetric Harmony

_Pages 21-21._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Symmetric Harmony defined

> Symetric Harmony differs greatly from Diatonic Harmony. There is no key sig-
> nature involved in Symetric Harmony and chords progress freely in this system of music.

**Significance (p. 21).** Foundational definition of the chapter's subject, distinguishing symmetric harmony from the diatonic system by its lack of key signature and free chord movement.

## Symmetric Harmony at the Cadence

> This harmony
> is introduced into the picture at the "Cadence". With endings you use the Symetric Har-
> mony at the tonic chord. In eight and sixteen bar sequences it is used at the "Cadence™
>
> breaks.

**Significance (p. 21).** Practical rule for when and where to apply symmetric harmony within a musical structure.

## Diminished 7th chord fingerboard movement

> In this lesson we have a diminished 7th chord which moves freely up and down the finger
> board in minorthirds. Exercise 31 illustrates the chord in its proper position all the way
> up the finger board.

**Significance (p. 21).** Introduces the central technique of the lesson — moving the diminished 7th chord in minor thirds along the fingerboard.

## Tonic notation system

> Notice that below each chord of the musical example there is
> a small "T"' and a number. The "T" represents the word, Tonic"; thenumber represents
> the tonic chord being employed.

**Significance (p. 21).** Defines the notation convention used throughout the lesson's examples.

## Every chord is the tonic

> As I mentioned before, there is no key in Symetric Harmony, therefore, every chord you
> play is, in actuality, the tonic. There can be as many tonics as you desire in Symetric
> Harmony.

**Significance (p. 21).** States the core theoretical principle of symmetric harmony — the absence of a hierarchical tonal center.

## 4-tonic division of 12-tone scale

> In order to avoid confusion, in this example and the examples to follow, we
> will use only 4 tonics; starting with “T1" and ending with "T1” anoctave higher. This di-
> vides the 12 tone scale into 4 equal parts.

**Significance (p. 21).** Establishes the practical working framework for the exercises — dividing the 12-tone scale into 4 equal parts.

## Diminished chord construction

> A diminished chord is built on a succession of minor thirds. If you play a diminished
> chord on the guitar and move it up a minor third, the same tones will appear but in a dif-
> ferent arrangement, which means, each tone in the chord is actually the root or the tonic
> (See Exercise 31b).

**Significance (p. 21).** Explains the intervallic construction of the diminished chord and its enharmonic equivalence across inversions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
